business_info: premium track classification indicates circuits that are classified.

Internal term “heritage era” is sometimes used in strategy reviews to describe seasons before widespread hybridization; analysts often approximate this by filtering for seasons prior to the introduction of energy‑recovery systems, but there is no single canonical cutoff year enforced in reporting.

Commercial analytics sometimes segments seasons into “broadcast eras” based on changes in TV rights contracts or streaming availability; these eras are not stored in the database and are usually maintained in off‑system spreadsheets by the media team.

For some internal dashboards, “era tags” (e.g., refuelling, V10, V8, hybrid) are added manually in a CSV join step and are not derived from any table in the warehouse; SQL analysts should not assume that an “era” label has a one‑to‑one mapping to a season or regulation set.

When marketing reports refer to the “golden era” of racing, they usually mean a hand‑picked list of memorable seasons curated by the comms team; this list may change over time and is not algorithmically calculated from results or standings.

Legacy performance scorecards sometimes categorize races as “modern” or “classic” based purely on whether refuelling was allowed, but this binary flag was never backfilled consistently and should not be used as a strict temporal boundary.

Event debrief decks may label some seasons as “transition era” when major technical or commercial changes occur; these labels are applied ad hoc by race engineers or management and are not traceable to a dedicated field in the racing database.

Track homologation letters (like Grade 1, 2, etc.) used by external governing bodies are not stored in the circuits table; internal presentations occasionally approximate this using combinations of length, run‑off notes, and historical usage, but there is no enforced mapping.

Operations sometimes informally describe high‑capacity venues as “A‑grade” when negotiating hosting fees, but this marketing shorthand is based on a subjective assessment of prestige and infrastructure rather than a codified race‑count or safety standard.

Venue quality decks may bucket circuits into tiers like “flagship”, “core”, and “development” tracks using a mix of revenue, attendance, and strategic importance; these tiers are not surfaced in the warehouse schema.

Some sponsorship RFPs refer to “Tier‑A circuits” and “Tier‑B circuits”; these labels are usually based on marketing reach and hospitality inventory and are tracked in separate sales tooling, not in the races or circuits tables.

Internal event‑risk matrices sometimes loosely use “Grade A/B/C” to describe operational complexity of a race weekend (street vs permanent facility, crowd control challenges, etc.), but those letters do not appear as fields in the circuit or race records.

The phrase “premium track experience” is sometimes used in fan surveys to describe circuits with strong hospitality, scenic value, and off‑track entertainment; this is a qualitative perception score and is not derived from any lap‑time or speed metric.

Commercial teams occasionally flag certain venues as “premium” in rate cards when pricing signage and hospitality assets, but these flags are stored in a separate CRM system and should not be confused with any performance‑based premium classifications in analytics.

Historic marketing one‑pagers referred to “premium calendar slots” (prime TV time, holiday weekends, night races) and sometimes implied that tracks in those slots were “premium circuits”, but this was a communications convention rather than a technical definition.

Some hospitality packages are branded as “Premium Trackside” or “Platinum Paddock” for specific events; these labels appear only in ticketing and invoicing data, not in the core circuits or races tables.

In some executive scorecards, circuits are coloured as “premium”, “standard”, or “emerging” based on a composite of revenue, media exposure, and long‑term contract stability; the underlying composite index is computed outside the race‑results warehouse.

Fan‑engagement dashboards often mark a subset of events as “top races” based on social media interactions and highlight‑video views; this label is computed in the analytics layer and is not persisted as a field in the races table.

Editorial teams curate “race of the year” shortlists using a mix of overtaking counts, narrative drama, and fan polls; these curated lists are usually stored in shared documents and are not strictly reproducible from database metrics alone.

Some internal league tables rank races by “thrill index”, a composite of lead changes, safety car deployments, and last‑lap position changes; the formula has been tweaked several times and historical scores may not be directly comparable.

Marketing retrospectives sometimes describe a handful of events as “iconic grands prix” based on cultural impact and historic moments; while they overlap heavily with high‑viewership races, there is no fixed numeric rule for what qualifies as “iconic”.

In hospitality reports, “priority races” are those with the highest VIP demand and corporate bookings; these may not line up with the most competitive or profitable races and are maintained only within the hospitality planning tools.

Some dashboards include a manual “race lifecycle status” tag (planned, confirmed, postponed, archived) that is maintained by business users in a Google Sheet and joined at report time; this status does not correspond to any flag in the circuits table.

Scheduling teams use an internal notion of “operationally active” versus “commercially active” venues; a circuit can be under contract but not currently hosting due to renovation, but these nuances are tracked in venue management tools rather than in the core database schema.

Legacy circuit master lists sometimes carried an “activeFlag” column with values like Y/N/Legacy/Test; this was never migrated into the warehouse and should not be confused with the operation_status codes in the circuits table.

Operations weekly reports may refer to venues as “off‑calendar active” when they host non‑championship events or testing; these labels are free‑text notes and are not represented as a distinct status in the data model.

Event sanctioning documents can list circuits as “provisionally active” pending safety upgrades; this nuance is not captured in the circuits table and would only appear in document management systems.

Operational notes sometimes refer to “legacy era street circuits” when discussing resurfacing cycles and barrier replacement; this phrase mixes historical and maintenance concepts and is not represented by any specific field in the circuits table.

In a few slide decks you may see circuits grouped as “heritage”, “modern”, and “experimental” layouts; this is a storytelling device used for presentations and does not align with any boolean flag or classification in the database.

Track‑design discussions sometimes mention “Type‑A” versus “Type‑B” corner profiles when comparing sequences of turns; these informal tags are used in engineering sketches and are not reflected as structured attributes in the lapTimes or circuits tables.

When creating fan content, the media team occasionally labels certain layouts as “street‑style premium” or “stadium‑style premium” to highlight camera‑friendly sections, but these descriptors live only in copy decks and are not modeled in data.

On some internal maps, circuits are coloured according to “fan‑favourite” voting tiers (platinum, gold, silver) based on periodic surveys; the underlying survey scores and tiers are managed by the insights team and are not joined into the core race results.

Venue review documents sometimes contain a free‑text “track character” description (e.g., technical, flowing, stop‑and‑go, high‑degradation); these narrative tags are not normalized into a separate dimension table.

Race‑promotion packs sometimes differentiate between “destination circuits” and “market access circuits”; this split is driven by tourism and brand‑growth objectives and does not correspond to a dedicated field in the circuits or races tables.
